instructions: Submit the room image and the desired paint colors.

title: Fabric Style Alignment
description: Adapts a draft fabric design to the aesthetic DNA of a style reference so it fits cohesively within a collection.
template:
Image 1 is a design draft for a fabric pattern. Image 2 is an existing design from the same collection that serves as the visual anchor for the desired art style.
**Objective:** Align the draft pattern (Image 1) to the aesthetic of the existing design (Image 2) while maintaining the original layout and structural intent of the draft.
**Focus Areas for Cohesion:**
- Linework & Texture: Adjust the stroke weight, taper, and graininess to match the reference.
- Color Profile: Harmonize the palette, ensuring the saturation and value contrast reflect Image 2.
- Level of Detail: Simplify or enhance the complexity of the motifs to ensure they feel like they were drawn by the same hand.
Carefully consider what elements need to change to achieve this cohesion, then generate the updated design.

variations.name: Preserve Shapes
variations.description: Shape-preserving style alignment.
variations.template:
Image 1 is a design draft for a fabric pattern. Image 2 is an existing design from the same collection that serves as the visual anchor for the desired art style.
**Objective:** Align the draft pattern (Image 1) to the aesthetic of the existing design (Image 2) while maintaining the original layout and structural intent of the draft.
**Focus Areas for Cohesion:**
- Linework & Texture: Adjust the stroke weight, taper, and graininess to match the reference.
- Color Profile: Harmonize the palette, ensuring the saturation and value contrast reflect Image 2.
- Level of Detail: Simplify or enhance the complexity of the motifs to ensure they feel like they were drawn by the same hand.
Carefully consider what elements need to change to achieve this cohesion, then generate the updated design.

variations.name: Style + Structure Changes
variations.description: Style-led motif and spacing reinterpretation.
variations.template:
Image 1 is a structural blueprint for a fabric pattern. Image 2 is the stylistic anchor.

**Objective:** Re-interpret the motifs and composition of Image 1 through the 'formal language' and structural logic of Image 2. The final result should look like a native piece of the Image 2 collection, using Image 1 only as a guide for placement and concept.

**Structural Adaptation (Priority):**

**Formal Morphology:** Do not copy the shapes in Image 1 literally. Instead, reshape the silhouettes, curves, and angles to match the 'hand' of Image 2. (e.g., if Image 2 is organic and fluid, soften any rigid structures from Image 1).

**Spatial Rhythm:** Adjust the spacing and 'breathing room' between elements to reflect the density and negative space found in Image 2.

**Weight & Balance:** Re-proportion the elements so the visual weight of the motifs aligns with the reference style.

**Aesthetic Cohesion:**

**Linework & Texture:** Mimic the specific stroke weight, taper, line quality, and tactile graininess of Image 2.

**Color & Value:** Harmonize the palette and contrast levels to be cohesive with the reference.

**Level of Abstraction:** Simplify or complicate the details of the motifs to match the artistic 'shorthand' used by the original artist in Image 2.

**Final Instruction:** Prioritize stylistic authenticity over literal structural fidelity to the draft. Generate the image.

instructions:
Submit with this prompt:
**Image 1:** Draft design
**Image 2:** Style reference

title: Extend Image Crop
description: Extends an uploaded fabric swatch beyond its current crop by additively continuing the composition.
template:
Aspect ratio: {{aspect}}

Extend the composition of the attached fabric swatch to meet the aspect ratio. Seamlessly continue the existing patterns, colors, and textures into the new space. The expansion must strictly respect the original design language, ensuring the transition is undetectable and that there is no perceptible change to the artistic style or material quality of the fabric. The result must be a high resolution, professional quality image that is ready for print in a high end magazine.

title: Technical Drawing Restyle
description: Transforms an uploaded technical drawing into a refined modernist graphic art print, preserving all original content while applying a hand-colored vintage arts-magazine aesthetic.
instructions: Select a variant to match your drawing subject.

variations.name: Musical Instrument
variations.description: Optimized for patent or technical drawings of musical instruments. Includes instrument-specific color guidance for wood bodies, strings, bands, and hardware.
variations.template:
Transform the uploaded technical drawing into a refined graphic art print while preserving the exact content.

Preserve all original diagrams, linework, labels, numbers, figure references, text, signatures, page margins, proportions, and technical relationships. Do not redraw the invention, alter geometry, simplify forms, invent missing parts, remove or replace text, add new annotations, or change any technical detail. Treat the existing drawing as fixed source material; only change the color treatment, surface quality, ink character, and overall graphic style.

Render the sheet as a tasteful modernist graphic interpretation of a vintage document, aligned with the look and feel of an early-to-mid 20th-century European arts magazine cover: hand-painted, editorial, slightly naive, confident, and collectible. The aesthetic should feel closer to a small-run illustrated arts journal or decorative avant-garde cover than to a conventional aged print. It should be historical and document-based, but with a fresher, more artistic color language than expected 1940s sepia, ochre, or blueprint tones.

Use a warm ivory paper ground with visible matte paper tooth, soft natural fibers, and very slight age toning. The surface should feel like thick vintage cover stock or rag paper: tactile, absorbent, and handmade, with mild edge wear and subtle tonal unevenness. Keep the background clean and gallery-ready. Avoid heavy staining, burned edges, torn paper effects, extreme foxing, or distressed grunge.

Convert the original black scan lines into crisp but human ink lines. Preserve legibility and all technical content. Use deep blue-black, charcoal-black, or inky aubergine for the linework, headings, figure labels, numbers, signatures, and legal text. Allow slight ink pooling, hand-pressure variation, and minor screen-print or lithographic irregularity, while keeping the drawing readable at large format.

Apply selective flat color fields in the spirit of the reference magazine cover: bold, hand-filled shapes with slightly uneven pigment density, softened edges, and visible brush or gouache texture. The color should feel painted onto the printed sheet, not digitally filled. Use color to create a strong decorative hierarchy while retaining the technical intelligence of the drawing.

For the primary body or large structural forms of the instrument, use one dominant flat color field that clearly separates the object from the paper ground. Favor a sophisticated but vivid modernist palette: coral red, vermilion-orange, mustard ochre, bottle green, aubergine-black, dusty rose and warm cream. The large body color should be tasteful and graphic, with subtle internal paper texture and pigment variation.

Use accent colors sparingly for key mechanical components: brass-like yellow for screws, knobs, and small hardware; bottle green for bands, strings, or structural divisions; mustard or green for secondary panels; vermilion or rose for a limited number of expressive curved or elongated elements. Keep these accents disciplined and compositional rather than illustrative.

Where the drawing shows wood bodies, casings, panels, or large structural masses, shade those areas differently from the background. Use a flat gouache-like color treatment with slight translucency, uneven edges, and faint paper grain showing through. The body should read as a designed form within the drawing, almost like an abstracted object on a vintage art-magazine cover, while the original linework and technical details remain intact.

Keep some areas uncolored. Let the original drawing breathe through the paper ground. Preserve large zones of negative space and use color only where it strengthens the composition: major body forms, principal assemblies, screws, springs, knobs, levers, and a few key mechanical details. The result should feel intentional, restrained, and artful rather than fully colored-in.

Maintain the full-page composition, centered and print-ready, with clean margins and the original orthographic presentation. The final image should look like an authentic technical sheet transformed into a collectible modernist graphic print: tactile, hand-colored, editorial, slightly avant-garde.

**Avoid:** heavy sepia, yellowed antique wash, blueprint styling, neon color, over-saturated pop art, cartoon effects, glossy digital gradients, painterly reinterpretation, decorative borders, added typography, changed text, altered labels, new objects, invented shadows, torn paper, burned edges, fake folds, extreme grunge, or any change to the technical content.

variations.name: Any Technical Drawing
variations.description: Works for any patent or technical drawing regardless of subject. Color guidance is intentionally non-specific, letting the model interpret the drawing's forms compositionally.
variations.template:
Transform the uploaded technical drawing into a refined graphic art print while preserving the exact content.

Preserve all original diagrams, linework, labels, numbers, figure references, text, signatures, page margins, proportions, and technical relationships. Do not alter geometry, simplify forms, remove text, or change any technical detail. Treat the drawing as fixed source material; change only the color treatment, surface quality, and ink character.

**Surface:** warm ivory paper ground with visible matte tooth and very slight age toning — thick vintage cover stock or rag paper, tactile and handmade, with mild edge wear and subtle tonal unevenness. No heavy staining, burned edges, extreme foxing, or distressed grunge.

Render scan lines as crisp but human ink lines using deep blue-black, charcoal-black, or inky aubergine. Allow slight ink pooling, hand-pressure variation, and minor lithographic irregularity. Keep all technical content fully legible. The overall aesthetic should evoke an early-to-mid 20th-century European arts magazine cover: hand-painted, editorial, slightly naive, confident, and collectible.

Apply selective flat color fields: bold, hand-filled shapes with slightly uneven pigment density and visible gouache or brush texture. Color should feel painted onto the sheet, not digitally filled. Use a sophisticated modernist palette: coral red, vermilion-orange, mustard ochre, bottle green, aubergine-black, dusty rose.

Color is graphic, not literal. In the spirit of early 20th-century poster design, color carries compositional and structural information rather than material truth. Shadows and tonal areas do not need naturalistic hues — a shadow can read convincingly as a shadow in a color that would never appear on the real object. Let color signal form, hierarchy, and visual weight.

Use one dominant flat color for the primary structural forms. Apply accent colors sparingly to secondary components, fasteners, panels, and key details. Keep large areas uncolored — let the paper ground breathe. The result should feel intentional and restrained, not fully colored-in.

Maintain the full-page composition, centered and print-ready, with clean margins and the original orthographic layout.

**Avoid:** heavy sepia, yellowed antique wash, blueprint styling, neon color, over-saturated pop art, cartoon effects, glossy digital gradients, painterly reinterpretation, decorative borders, added typography, changed text, altered labels, new objects, invented shadows, torn paper, burned edges, fake folds, extreme grunge, or any change to the technical content.

title: Topographic Site Plan
description: Recreates an uploaded map, satellite view, or site-plan screenshot as a hand-colored historical topographic site plan using a fixed manuscript-map style reference.
instructions: Upload an overhead map, satellite view, or site-plan screenshot as the Site Reference, then choose the desired historical map style variant. Each variant includes its own fixed Style Reference.

variations.name: Manuscript Site Plan
variations.description: Early nineteenth-century hand-colored manuscript plan.
variations.instructions: Upload an overhead map, satellite view, or site-plan screenshot as the Site Reference. The fixed manuscript-map Style Reference is included automatically.
variations.template:
I have uploaded an image labeled or intended as the Site Reference: an overhead map, satellite view, or site plan of a real area. Recreate that site as a hand-colored historical topographic site plan, using the uploaded Site Reference as the sole geographic reference and the permanent Style Reference at {{topographic_site_plan_style}} as the sole stylistic reference.

Preserve the actual modern site relationships from the Site Reference: land parcel boundaries, primary building footprints, neighboring building masses, street alignments, adjacent streets, curb geometry, sidewalk and paved open areas, planted or residual open spaces, and the relative spacing between all major elements. Preserve road geometry exactly, including irregular curves, bends, offsets, dead ends, cul-de-sacs, angled intersections, and non-parallel alignments; do not simplify winding streets into clean loops or regularized diagram shapes. If elevation, slope, shoreline, landscape edges, or grade relationships are legible in the Site Reference, express them with restrained brown contour, hachure, or wash cues; otherwise do not invent topography. Do not invent a different plan, change the site layout, regularize irregular streets, or replace the real urban relationships with generic map geometry.

Translate the site into an early nineteenth-century manuscript plan aesthetic. Use pale cream laid paper with subtle age toning, faint edge wear, and a thin hand-ruled border. Draw all geometry in fine black-brown ink with slight hand irregularity, measured linework, and restrained archival softness.

Apply transparent watercolor washes: muted rose-pink for surrounding blocks or building fields, warm ivory for streets and open ground, pale gray-blue only for paved roadway areas where it improves hierarchy, and restrained green for planted or open residual spaces. Keep color flat, translucent, softly uneven, and subordinate to the plan drawing.

Represent the primary site, parcel, or building as the central mapped subject with a clean measured footprint, light ochre or cream fill, darker ink outline, and a small handwritten label. Label major streets in delicate slanted cursive handwriting. Add sparse cartographic annotations, small directional arrows, parcel edges, curb lines, path edges, and tiny tree, bollard, or planting marks only where they clarify the site. Include a small page number, archival stamp, and faint catalog notation in the margins.

Maintain plan-view orthographic logic, flattened perspective, measured spacing, and readable hierarchy. The final image should feel like a historically plausible archival manuscript plan of the actual uploaded site, not a decorative fantasy map.

Avoid modern map symbols, pins, app labels, UI colors, satellite imagery, photorealism, heavy texture, crisp vector perfection, decorative fantasy elements, excessive aging, fictional landmarks, ornate compass roses, 3D perspective, and any geographic changes that contradict the Site Reference.

variations.name: Balloon Prospect Map
variations.description: Antiquarian balloon-prospect engraving.
variations.instructions: Upload a topographic view, site plan, map, satellite view, or overhead site image as the Site Reference. The fixed balloon-prospect Style Reference is included automatically.
variations.template:
Use the uploaded Site Reference as the geographic source. Use this Style Reference as the dominant visual reference for medium, palette, composition logic, engraving texture, cloud treatment, border, captioning, atmosphere, and archival presentation:
{{balloon_prospect_style_reference}}

Transform the Site Reference into a hand-colored antiquarian balloon-prospect map: an elevated overhead or high bird's-eye cartographic view seen as if from above the clouds. Preserve the real site, plan, map, or topographic relationships from the Site Reference: landform shapes, water edges, road and path geometry, parcel or block structure, building footprints, trails, vegetation masses, slopes, boundaries, labels if essential, and the relative spacing between major elements. The Site Reference controls geography and factual layout; the Style Reference controls visual language. Preserve permanent geography and large built-form relationships, but remove modern transient objects and visible modern infrastructure from the final antique interpretation.

Adopt the Style Reference's composition logic without copying its specific geography: a vertically or horizontally framed engraved plate as appropriate to the source, dark green land masses broken by pale cloud banks, salmon-pink roads or routes winding through the site, blue-black or dark green shadowed recesses, and a strong hand-ruled border with a lower printed caption strip or margin when composition allows. Let clouds behave as atmospheric overlays and framing devices; they may partly veil nonessential open areas, but they must not hide the site-defining geometry needed to recognize the uploaded source.

Render the image as a late eighteenth- or early nineteenth-century hand-colored engraving on aged paper. Use dense engraved hatching, stipple, crosshatch, and fine directional line texture throughout the land, clouds, shadows, and water. Keep edges slightly worn and printed rather than digitally crisp. Use dark bottle green, black-green, moss green, smoky gray, warm cream, off-white, faded salmon, dusty rose, muted ochre, and occasional blue-black. Color should feel hand-applied over an engraved plate: uneven, rubbed, transparent in places, and darkened by age.

For terrain and vegetation, use granular engraved marks, clustered tree textures, stippled fields, and dark shaded masses. For paths, roads, waterways, routes, or ridgelines, use the Style Reference's pale salmon or faded peach linear language where appropriate, following the actual geometry of the Site Reference rather than inventing decorative curves. Translate modern roads, parking lots, drive aisles, and paved areas into plain antique route or ground shapes without lane striping, painted parking lines, crosswalk bars, traffic arrows, curbs marked as modern engineering detail, or road signage. For clouds, use large rounded cream-white cloud forms with gray engraved modeling, soft smoky undersides, and visible hatch texture.

Include antiquarian print presentation where appropriate: plate impression, double ruled border, faint page crease or age marks, small italic page-note text or plate notation near an upper margin, and a lower caption band with loose period-style lettering. Any caption or lettering should remain atmospheric unless the Site Reference contains specific text that must be preserved.

Remove anachronistic detail while keeping the site legible: omit cars, trucks, parking stalls, street lane lines, crosswalks, traffic signs, utility poles and wires, rooftop HVAC units, exterior air-conditioning equipment, vents, solar panels, dumpsters, loading docks, modern street furniture, contemporary lighting, and other mechanical or vehicular clutter. Replace these with generalized engraved land, roof, path, vegetation, cloud, or shadow texture consistent with the Style Reference. Do not preserve modern objects merely because they appear in the Site Reference; preserve only the durable geographic, topographic, route, parcel, landscape, water, and major building relationships.

Before finalizing, perform an internal style fidelity and anachronism assessment against the Style Reference. Check medium, palette, composition logic, cloud reserves, engraving hatch texture, line/edge behavior, aged paper, border/caption format, hand-colored print quality, and absence of modern objects or infrastructure. If the result looks like a modern map, clean vector diagram, generic watercolor, fantasy map, satellite repaint, or a watered-down version of the Style Reference, or if it still contains cars, lane markings, HVAC/AC systems, utility clutter, or parking-lot markings, revise internally by strengthening the engraving texture, cloud masses, dark green/salmon palette, border/caption presentation, and anti-modern constraints while preserving the same durable Site Reference geography.

Avoid modern map pins, app labels, UI colors, satellite imagery, photorealism, clean vector styling, flat digital illustration, glossy 3D terrain, fantasy-map symbols, ornate compass roses, fictional landmarks, arbitrary decorative routes, invented geography, cars, trucks, street lane markings, parking-space stripes, crosswalks, traffic arrows, traffic signs, rooftop HVAC units, air-conditioning systems, vents, solar panels, utility poles or wires, dumpsters, modern streetlights, excessive readability of fake text, and any geographic changes that contradict the durable Site Reference layout.

referenceImages.label: Balloon Prospect Style Reference
referenceImages.instructions: Use this reference for hand-colored engraving texture, cloud banks, dark green land masses, salmon route/path color, aged paper, ruled border, printed caption margin, and antiquarian balloon-prospect presentation. Do not copy its specific geography or caption unless present in the uploaded Site Reference.

title: Miniature Portrait
description: Creates a formal historical bust portrait by combining an uploaded character likeness with a fixed 19th-century American folk-art miniature style reference.
template:
Create a formal historical bust portrait combining the likeness from the attached 'character reference' image and the artistic style from the style reference at {{miniature_style}}.

**Character & Likeness:** The portrait must feature the precise facial features, expression, and likeness of the subject shown in the character reference image, seamlessly integrated.

**Artistic Style & Medium:** Apply the exact 19th-century American Folk Art watercolor and gouache painting technique seen in the style reference. This includes the precise brushwork, the soft tonal washes, the use of desaturated, rich pigments, and the distinct, hand-painted texture. The background must be the same aged, textured cream paper with its specific patina.

**Composition & Attire:** Replicate the formal, three-quarter bust composition and the exact attire (the dark charcoal-grey frock coat, the white multiple-buttoned waistcoat, and the oversized black silk bow tie stock) from the style reference, but adapted to the character's form. Maintain the neutral lighting and composed, formal expression.
